morphology.summary: A Latinate form connected to root-related meaning plus an adjectival suffix.

etymology.summary: The lexical family is connected to Latin radix, meaning root.

semantic_drift.summary: The meaning moves from root-related to fundamental, reformist, extreme, and sometimes informal praise.

developments.description: Root imagery supports abstract foundation-oriented senses.
Fundamental change becomes associated with reform movements.
Intensity and unconventionality support informal approval.

present_usage.summary: Modern usage includes fundamental, politically transformative, extreme, and informal positive senses.

senses.definition: Relating to the root, basis, or foundation of something.

examples.text: The team proposed a radical redesign rather than a cosmetic fix.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

senses.definition: Favoring deep or far-reaching social or political change.

examples.text: The speech sounded radical to voters who preferred gradual reform.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

examples.text: That was a radical solution to a hard problem.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

cultural_frame.summary: The word crosses technical, political, and informal frames.

frames.label: root-cause analysis
frames.description: Frames an intervention as going below symptoms to foundations.

frames.label: political transformation
frames.description: Frames change as either necessary transformation or dangerous upheaval.

pragmatics.summary: Radical can be neutral, admiring, alarming, or casually approving.

stances.label: technical precision
stances.description: Marks a claim as reaching foundations.

stances.label: warning
stances.description: Marks change as too extreme or destabilizing.

stances.label: praise
stances.description: Marks change as bold or transformative.

expansion.paragraph: Radical should not be reduced to extreme. Its root etymology supports meanings about foundations and root causes. Political usage extends that root-level sense to deep reform, while evaluative and informal uses can make it alarming, admiring, or casually positive.

trace.contribution: The lexical family is connected to Latin radix, meaning root.
The meaning moves from root-related to fundamental, reformist, extreme, and sometimes informal praise.
Radical can be neutral, admiring, alarming, or casually approving.

capsule_summary: A root-derived word whose meanings range from fundamental to politically transformative, extreme, or informally excellent.

provenance.citation: Wiktionary contributors, "radical", Wiktionary, The Free Dictionary.

provenance.note: Used as a cited lexical source; capsule wording is manually normalized and layered.

provenance.citation: Etymonoetic Interlingua curated analysis for radical.
provenance.license: MIT for original EI analysis; source-derived facts retain their source terms.
provenance.note: Manual layer separation, pragmatic analysis, examples, and expansion.

unresolved_questions: Split scientific, mathematical, medical, and political senses into narrower capsules later.
